toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
#
# Recipe Bottle base compose file — static infrastructure
#
# Invoked via:
#   docker compose --env-file .rbk/rbrr.env --env-file .rbk/{moniker}/rbrn.env \
#     -f .rbk/rbob_compose.yml [-f .rbk/{moniker}/compose.yml] -p {moniker} up -d
#
# Env-file variables serve dual purpose:
#   1. YAML interpolation (${VAR} in this file — image refs, subnet, container names)
#   2. Container environment forwarding (bare names in environment: sections)
#
# Network topology:
#   sentry: transit (uplink to internet via bridge) + enclave (down to bottle)
#     Interface naming and default-route assignment are NOT controlled by compose:
#       - eth0/eth1 ordering is Docker-engine-defined (sentry IP-discovers via RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP)
#       - default route is assigned to the alphabetically-first attached network's gateway
#         (compose 'priority:' keys empirically do NOT influence this on linux Docker
#         engine 28.x or mac Docker Desktop 28.x — sentry replaces the default route
#         with one via uplink at RBJp1, see rbjs_sentry.sh)
#   pentacle: enclave only (static IP, routes through sentry)
#   bottle: shares pentacle network namespace (network_mode: service:pentacle)
#
# Health chain: sentry healthy → pentacle starts → pentacle healthy → bottle starts (sessile profile)

services:
  sentry:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/rbi_hm/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-sentry
    privileged: true
    networks:
      transit: {}
      enclave:
        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}
    environment:
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP
      - RBRR_DNS_SERVER
      - RBRN_ENTRY_MODE
      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION
      - RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_DNS_MODE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_PORT_MIN
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_CIDRS
      - RBRN_UPLINK_ALLOWED_DOMAINS
      - RBJE_PROBE
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjh_healthy"]
      interval: 2s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 15
      start_period: 5s

  pentacle:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/rbi_hm/${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-pentacle
    privileged: true
    networks:
      enclave:
        ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}
    environment:
      - RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP
    command: ["/opt/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh"]
    depends_on:
      sentry:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "test -f /tmp/rbjph_healthy"]
      interval: 2s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 15
      start_period: 5s

  bottle:
    image: ${RBRR_GCP_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${RBDC_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID}/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}/rbi_hm/${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}/image:${RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK}
    container_name: ${RBRR_RUNTIME_PREFIX}${RBRN_MONIKER}-bottle
    network_mode: "service:pentacle"
    security_opt:
      - label:disable
    cap_drop:
      - NET_RAW
    environment:
      - RBOB_HOST_UID
      - RBOB_HOST_GID
      - RBRV_USER
    depends_on:
      pentacle:
        condition: service_healthy
    profiles:
      - sessile

networks:
  transit:
    driver: bridge
  enclave:
    driver: bridge
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}

file.content:
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p icmp -j ACCEPT || exit 20

echo "RBJp2: Phase 2: Port Setup"
if test "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}" = "enabled"; then
  echo "RBJp2: Configuring entry port via iptables DNAT (no userspace proxy)"

  echo "RBJp2: Enabling IP forwarding for entry port DNAT"
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward || exit 25

  echo "RBJp2: DNAT incoming connections on entry port to bottle"
  # Source-IP exclusion (not -i UPLINK_IF) encodes the actual invariant:
  # entry-port traffic originating outside the enclave subnet. Decouples from
  # Docker's choice of port-publish delivery interface (alphabetical fallback,
  # see header comment of rbob_compose.yml).
  iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}" \
           ! -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
           -j DNAT --to-destination "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" || exit 25

  echo "RBJp2: Allow forwarding of DNATted entry traffic to bottle"
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" \
           ! -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}" \
           -j ACCEPT || exit 25

  echo "RBJp2: MASQUERADE return traffic so bottle replies to sentry"
  iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -o ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -p tcp \
           -d "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}" --dport "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}" -j MASQUERADE || exit 25
fi

echo "RBJp2b: Blocking ICMP cross-boundary traffic"
iptables -A RBM-FORWARD         -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28
iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -p icmp -j DROP || exit 28

echo "RBJp3: Phase 3: Access Setup"
if test "${RBRN_UPLINK_ACCESS_MODE}" = "disabled"; then
  echo "RBJp3: Blocking all non-port traffic"
  iptables -A RBM-EGRESS  -o ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
  iptables -A RBM-FORWARD -i ${RBJ_ENCLAVE_IF} -j DROP || exit 30
else
  echo "RBJp3: Setting up network forwarding"
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward               || exit 31
  echo 0 > /proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/disable_ipv6    || exit 31
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/all/rp_filter       || exit 31
  echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/conf/${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}/route_localnet || exit 31

input.params: {"identity": "A_AAb", "files": [".rbk/rbob_compose.yml", "rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh"], "intent": "Topology-level reframe of the entry port: move port publish from sentry (dual-network) to pentacle (single-network), and delete the entry-port iptables block from sentry. The architectural flaw being repaired: making sentry the publisher coupled the gatekeeper role with a multi-network disambiguation problem that has no portable Compose/runtime API. Pentacle is single-attached (enclave only); Docker has one network to target, no disambiguation. Pentacle and bottle share a network namespace, so the publish lands on the bottle's listener directly. Compose change: ports directive moves from sentry to pentacle with asymmetric form ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE} (host 7999 → in-namespace 8000 for srjcl, 7999 → 8080 for pluml). The asymmetric form is necessary because without sentry's PREROUTING DNAT the publish itself must carry the port translation. Sentry script change: the entire entry-mode if-block at lines 117-142 deleted (PREROUTING DNAT, RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE — all three become moot when sentry is not in the inbound path). ip_forward enablement was previously latent in the entry-mode block; lifted to an unconditional position at RBJp2 since sentry forwards on behalf of the enclave whenever access mode permits, independent of entry mode. Removed the redundant ip_forward set in the access-mode-enabled branch (was duplicated). Security posture: ifrit's net-dnat-entry-reflection invariant ('enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry entry port') is preserved trivially — sentry no longer has an entry port. Sentry's egress-gatekeeper role unchanged (default route, DNS, FORWARD/EGRESS rules for outbound traffic all untouched). Next: kludge sentry vessel, refresh hallmarks on srjcl + pluml + tadmor (all three exercised in parallel verification), run the three fixtures in parallel."}

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.

# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# RBOO - Recipe Bottle Orchestration Observe
# Network observation (tcpdump) for crucibles
#
# Requires: buc_command.sh sourced
# Requires: rbob_bottle.sh sourced and kindled

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBOO_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBOO_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Kindle and Sentinel

zrboo_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBOO_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rboo already kindled"

  # Verify RBOB is kindled (provides container names, runtime)
  zrbob_sentinel

  # Terminal control sequences for colored output (ANSI literals, matching BUC constants)
  readonly ZRBOO_BOLD=$'\033[1m'
  readonly ZRBOO_YELLOW=$'\033[33m'
  readonly ZRBOO_BLUE=$'\033[34m'
  readonly ZRBOO_WHITE=$'\033[37m'
  readonly ZRBOO_RESET=$'\033[0m'

  # Common tcpdump options: unbuffered, line-buffered, no name resolution, verbose
  ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS=(-U -l -nn -vvv)
  readonly ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS

82	######################################################################
83	# Public API
84	
85	# Observe network traffic on crucible containers
86	# Runs until Ctrl+C; all captures run in parallel
87	rboo_observe() {
88	  zrboo_sentinel
89	
90	  buc_step "Starting network observation: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"
91	
92	  # Set up cleanup trap
93	  trap 'buc_info "Stopping captures..."; kill 0 2>/dev/null; exit 0' SIGINT SIGTERM
94	
95	  buc_info "Network topology:"
96	  buc_info "  SENTRY: Bridge (eth0) <-> Internet, Enclave (eth1) <-> Internal"
97	  buc_info "  PENTACLE/BOTTLE: Shared namespace on Enclave network (eth0)"
98	
99	  # Start pentacle capture (shared namespace with bottle)
100	  buc_info "Starting pentacle/bottle capture (eth0)"

}

######################################################################
# Public API

# Observe network traffic on crucible containers
# Runs until Ctrl+C; all captures run in parallel
rboo_observe() {
  zrboo_sentinel

  buc_step "Starting network observation: ${RBRN_MONIKER}"

  # Set up cleanup trap
  trap 'buc_info "Stopping captures..."; kill 0 2>/dev/null; exit 0' SIGINT SIGTERM

  buc_info "Network topology:"
  buc_info "  SENTRY: Bridge (eth0) <-> Internet, Enclave (eth1) <-> Internal"
  buc_info "  PENTACLE/BOTTLE: Shared namespace on Enclave network (eth0)"

  # Start pentacle capture (shared namespace with bottle)
  buc_info "Starting pentacle/bottle capture (eth0)"
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_PENTACLE}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth0 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_pentacle &

  # Start sentry enclave capture
  buc_info "Starting sentry enclave capture (eth1)"
  "${ZRBOB_RUNTIME}" exec "${ZRBOB_SENTRY}" tcpdump "${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS[@]}" -i eth1 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_sentry &

  # Bridge capture: only for podman (requires podman machine ssh)
  if test "${RBRN_RUNTIME}" = "podman"; then
    # Discover bridge interface for enclave network
    z_rboo_bridge_interface=$(${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} network inspect "${ZRBOB_NETWORK}" --format '{{.NetworkInterface}}')
    buc_info "Starting bridge capture (${z_rboo_bridge_interface}) via podman machine ssh"
    ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} machine ssh "sudo -n tcpdump ${ZRBOO_TCPDUMP_OPTS} -i ${z_rboo_bridge_interface}" 2>&1 | zrboo_prefix_bridge &
  else
    buc_info "Bridge capture not available for Docker runtime (requires podman machine ssh)"
  fi

  buc_info "Press Ctrl+C to stop captures"

  # Wait for all background processes
  wait
}

# eof

content.text:
**Scry tooling found.** Summary:

- **Tabtarget**: `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh <nameplate>` (e.g. `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh srjcl`)
- **Implementation**: `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh:rboo_observe()`
- **What it does**: runs `tcpdump -U -l -nn -vvv` inside pentacle on `eth0` (shared with bottle) AND inside sentry on `eth1` (uplink), in parallel, prefixed for stream identification.
- **Interactive**: runs until Ctrl+C, then SIGINT cleans up the captures.
- **Critical for our case**: pentacle's `eth0` is the enclave interface in the bottle's namespace. Anything arriving at the published port (now routed via pentacle) shows up there with source IP + destination port preserved as the bottle's listener sees it.

For our diagnostic: charge srjcl, start scry in one background stream, curl from another, observe what shows up in the pentacle capture. That will tell us definitively:
- Are packets arriving at pentacle's eth0 at all?
- If yes, what source IP?
- If yes, what destination port?

**Current failure status:**

| Fixture | Result |
|---|---|
| srjcl | `_jupyter_running` ✓, `_jupyter_connectivity` ✗ |
| pluml | `_text_rendering` ✗ (first case probes published port → bottle) |
| tadmor | still running |

So **the pentacle-publisher topology also fails on macOS** — same surface-level symptom as before (curia→published port→bottle listener not connecting). The pentacle-as-single-network theory predicted this should work; the empirical result says it doesn't. We need scry to see what's actually happening before guessing further.

I'll wait for tadmor to complete, then propose: charge srjcl, start scry, curl, observe — single short diagnostic loop.

| Fixture | Result |
|---|---|
| **srjcl** | `_jupyter_running` ✓, `_jupyter_connectivity` ✗ |
| **pluml** | `_text_rendering` ✗ (first case) |

**What we learned:**
- Tadmor green proves the sentry script changes (entry-port block deleted, `ip_forward` lifted) don't break the egress-gatekeeper role. The architectural posture for outbound traffic is intact under the new image.
- srjcl and pluml break on the inbound published-port path. The pentacle-as-single-network-publisher hypothesis predicted this should work; the empirical reality says it doesn't, at least not on macOS Docker Desktop.

**What we don't yet know:** why. Possible candidates (none verified):
- Docker Desktop's port-publish to a service whose namespace is shared via `network_mode: service:...` has semantics different from expected (maybe it doesn't proxy through to the shared-namespace listener).
- Pentacle's interface state at publish-target time isn't what we assume (e.g., the `ip link set down/up` flush at `rbjp_pentacle.sh:44` happens after compose has already bound the publish target — race condition?).
- Some other path-related issue we haven't theorized.

We have three live commits on this experiment (compose move, sentry script reframe, three nameplate hallmark refreshes — 5 commits total from `c8b218ef` onward). Rollback is straightforward but not done yet.

Holding for your decision.

message.content: Can you provide a more detailed statement of issue as a prompt to my local architect?  This is a breaking finding, I think.

content.text:
Here's a self-contained prompt for your architect — covers premise, every fix attempted, the empirical record, and the narrowed question.

---

# Pentacle-Publisher Reframe — Architectural Failure Mode

## Premise

In this repo, a `crucible` is a three-container Compose project: **sentry** (privileged, dual-attached to `transit` + `enclave` networks; runs iptables + dnsmasq), **pentacle** (privileged, attached to `enclave` only; configures bottle's default route via sentry), and **bottle** (inherits pentacle's namespace via `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`; runs the actual service). For nameplates with `RBRN_ENTRY_MODE=enabled` (srjcl Jupyter, pluml PlantUML), a host port is published so the curia (host) can reach the bottle's listener.

Original regression (memo `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md`): `rbtdrc_srjcl_jupyter_connectivity` returns HTTP 000. Root cause: published port lived on sentry; sentry's iptables DNAT was scoped to the uplink interface; commit ₢BBABC renamed compose network `default → transit`, which made `enclave` alphabetically-first; Docker began delivering port-publish via the enclave interface, where the DNAT didn't match. Symptom is empirical on both linux Engine 28.x and macOS Desktop 28.x.

## Repair attempts and outcomes

**Attempt 1 — Compose rename `transit → auplink`** (experiment, reverted).
Made the uplink network alphabetically first. Restored old delivery path. srjcl green with original `-i UPLINK_IF` rule. Confirmed the alphabetical mechanism. Rejected as the canonical fix because it depends on an undocumented Docker heuristic.

**Attempt 2 — Source-IP exclusion on sentry iptables**.
Replaced `-i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}` with `! -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}"` on PREROUTING DNAT and RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT. Premise: encode "not enclave-originated" as the actual semantic invariant rather than using interface name as a proxy.
- *Stage 1 (rename + fix)*: srjcl 3/3 ✓, pluml 5/5 ✓, tadmor 54/54 ✓.
- *Stage 2 (no rename, fix alone)*: srjcl `_connectivity` ✗, pluml 5/5 ✓ (cases don't exercise published port), tadmor 54/54 ✓.

Hypothesis for stage 2 failure (unverified): when Docker delivers via the enclave interface, the source IP after host-side SNAT is the enclave bridge gateway IP (e.g., `10.242.2.1` for srjcl whose enclave is `10.242.2.0/24`), which IS inside the enclave CIDR — so `! -s ENCLAVE_CIDR` blocks legitimate traffic. **Not empirically confirmed; we did not run a tcpdump probe before moving on.**

**Attempt 3 — Pentacle-publisher topology reframe** (current state on disk).
Premise: the disambiguation problem only exists because sentry is multi-attached AND hosts the published port. Move `ports:` to pentacle (single-attached, enclave only). Docker then has exactly one network to target. Bottle's listener (in pentacle's namespace) receives directly. Delete the entire entry-port iptables block from sentry — it's unnecessary if sentry isn't on the inbound path.

Concrete edits (commits `c8b218ef` and successors):

`.rbk/rbob_compose.yml`:
```yaml
# was on sentry service:
#   ports:
#     - "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}"
# now on pentacle service:
    ports:
      - "${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}"
```
(Asymmetric mapping `7999:8000` because sentry's DNAT no longer does port translation; the publish itself carries it.)

`rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`: deleted the entire `if test "${RBRN_ENTRY_MODE}" = "enabled"; then ... fi` block (PREROUTING DNAT, RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE). Lifted `echo 1 > /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward` to an unconditional position at RBJp2. Removed redundant `ip_forward` set in access-mode-enabled branch.

Test results after kludge + hallmark refresh on srjcl/pluml/tadmor, parallel three-fixture run on macOS Docker Desktop 28.x:

| Fixture | Result |
|---|---|
| srjcl | `_jupyter_running` ✓ (bottle process up, `ps aux` confirms jupyter-lab bound to `0.0.0.0:8000`), `_jupyter_connectivity` ✗ (curia `curl http://localhost:7999/lab` → HTTP 000) |
| pluml | `_text_rendering` ✗ (first case, same shape: curia → published port → bottle listener) |
| tadmor | 54/54 ✓ — including `rbtdrc_sortie_net_dnat_entry_reflection` (the ifrit invariant gate) |

**Tadmor green confirms the sentry script changes don't break the egress-gatekeeper posture.** The architectural intent for outbound traffic is intact.

**srjcl/pluml red confirms the pentacle-publisher hypothesis is empirically false on macOS Docker Desktop**, contrary to the structural reasoning.

## The narrowed question

Compose declares `pentacle.ports: - "7999:8000"`. Pentacle is single-attached to the `enclave` network with `ipv4_address: ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BOTTLE_IP}` (e.g., `10.242.2.3`). Bottle has `network_mode: "service:pentacle"`, shares the namespace, and binds `0.0.0.0:8000`.

The expected behavior: host `curl localhost:7999` → docker-proxy → pentacle's network endpoint at `10.242.2.3:8000` → bottle's jupyter listener → HTTP 200.

The actual behavior on macOS Desktop: HTTP 000.

**Why doesn't this work?**

Candidate hypotheses (none verified — we have not yet run scry/tcpdump on the failing path):

1. **`network_mode: service:...` + `ports:` on the namespace owner is silently unsupported on Docker Desktop.** The shared-namespace listener may not be reachable through the published-port proxy that targets the namespace owner.
2. **Pentacle's `ip link set ${RBJP_ENCLAVE_IF} down && up` at `rbjp_pentacle.sh:44` invalidates Docker's port-publish proxy backend.** Compose binds the proxy at container start; the flush happens during pentacle's init script, *after* compose has cached the endpoint. Subsequent connections to the proxy may target a stale endpoint.
3. **Healthcheck/dependency ordering**: bottle starts only after pentacle is `service_healthy`. The published-port proxy is wired to pentacle's container at container-create time, not at health-ready time. If the bottle's listener hadn't bound by the time the test probes, we'd expect `_jupyter_running` to also fail — but it passes. So this is probably not it.
4. **Docker Desktop's userspace networking interposition** treats the pentacle-namespace target differently than a service that owns its own namespace (independent of the shared-namespace question).
5. **Conntrack/iptables interaction inside pentacle's namespace** (pentacle inherits sentry's image, but pentacle runs `rbjp_pentacle.sh` not `rbjs_sentry.sh` — so it does NOT install iptables rules; it only sets DNS, flushes ARP, and adds a default route via sentry). Could the default route via sentry's enclave IP be intercepting return traffic that should otherwise go out via Docker's proxy?

Hypothesis 5 is the one I find structurally most plausible:

> Bottle binds `0.0.0.0:8000`. docker-proxy delivers `host:7999 → 10.242.2.3:8000` from the host side via the enclave bridge gateway. The bottle's listener accepts and SYN-ACKs. **Return packet's destination is the source IP of the incoming connection (some host-side address from outside the enclave). Bottle's routing table has `default via ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP}` (set by pentacle's script).** So the SYN-ACK is sent to sentry's enclave IP, not back to docker-proxy. Sentry tries to forward it out the uplink interface where it has no return route. Connection never completes. HTTP 000.

If hypothesis 5 is the explanation, the pentacle's "all traffic via sentry" routing is fundamentally incompatible with direct port-publish to the bottle: the inbound path is direct, but the return path is forced through sentry's uplink — a routing asymmetry. Sentry's old DNAT+MASQUERADE wasn't just doing destination translation; it was making the return path *symmetric* by sourcing the bottle-side connection from sentry itself.

This would mean the architectural premise of attempt 3 was incomplete: it's not enough to put the publisher on a single-network container if that container's default route forces outbound through a different exit point than the inbound entered.

## What the architect should produce

One of:

**(A) Empirical disambiguation plan.** We have `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh <nameplate>` which runs tcpdump inside pentacle on `eth0` and inside sentry on `eth1` simultaneously. A single curl probe with scry running should reveal: do packets arrive at pentacle? With what source IP? Does the bottle send a SYN-ACK? Does that SYN-ACK arrive at sentry's enclave interface (hypothesis 5) or somewhere else? Propose a specific capture-and-probe sequence and what each possible observation maps to in terms of root cause.

**(B) A structural alternative that survives the routing-asymmetry problem.** If hypothesis 5 holds, candidates:
- Have the bottle (or pentacle) install per-source-IP routing rules so return packets to non-enclave sources go via the bridge gateway, not via sentry.
- Have sentry MASQUERADE inbound external traffic so the bottle sees sentry as the source, making the return symmetric again (this was effectively what the old design did, just via a more convoluted path).
- Topology change: separate publisher container that's truly one-network and doesn't share the bottle's default-via-sentry route.

**(C) A reason this analysis is wrong.** If the failing path is something other than hypothesis 5, identify what's actually breaking and propose a fix.

## Constraints

- Cross-runtime: must eventually work on Docker Engine, Docker Desktop, and Podman (rootful + rootless). Heuristic-dependent fixes are not acceptable as canonical.
- Security posture: ifrit's `net-dnat-entry-reflection` invariant ("enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry's entry port") must be preserved. The reframe preserved it trivially by removing sentry's entry port; alternative solutions must preserve it explicitly.
- Sentry's egress-gatekeeper role (bottle's default route through sentry, sentry's DNS, sentry's outbound FORWARD chain) is canonical and must remain.

## Source pointers

- `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md` — full diagnostic history including Refined Fix Shape (now superseded by attempt 3) and cross-runtime portability matrix.
- `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml` — current compose (pentacle owns `ports:`).
- `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh` — current sentry init (entry-port block deleted, `ip_forward` lifted to RBJp2).
- `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjp_pentacle.sh` — pentacle init (default route via sentry's enclave IP at line 47).
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs:1597-1646` — srjcl test case definitions.
- `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/rbida_sorties.rs:1961-2048` — `net-dnat-entry-reflection` ifrit invariant.
- `Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh` — scry implementation (tcpdump in pentacle + sentry, runs until Ctrl+C).
- Latest reframe commits: `c8b218ef` (compose+script), `8798c6a4`/`3c5bac2d`/`ba8518fc` (per-nameplate hallmark refreshes).

content.text: Important observation: sentry's RBM-FORWARD has DNS+allowlist-CIDR rules only — no catch-all. Any forward traffic NOT to `160.79.104.0/23` (Anthropic allowlist) or DNS hits the default DROP. Now the tcpdump+curl probe.

input.command:
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle timeout 8 tcpdump -U -l -nn -i any -c 50 > /tmp/scry-pentacle.txt 2>&1 &
PENTACLE_PID=$!
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-sentry   timeout 8 tcpdump -U -l -nn -i any -c 50 > /tmp/scry-sentry.txt   2>&1 &
SENTRY_PID=$!
sleep 2
curl --connect-timeout 3 --max-time 5 -v http://localhost:7999/lab > /tmp/scry-curl.txt 2>&1
sleep 3
wait $PENTACLE_PID 2>/dev/null
wait $SENTRY_PID 2>/dev/null
echo "=== CURL ==="
cat /tmp/scry-curl.txt
echo ""
echo "=== PENTACLE TCPDUMP ==="
cat /tmp/scry-pentacle.txt
echo ""
echo "=== SENTRY TCPDUMP ==="
cat /tmp/scry-sentry.txt

content: Okay to add tcpdump to sentry, commit, kludge, commit.

content.text:
**SMOKING GUN found in conntrack:**

```
SYN_RECV  src=192.168.65.1  dst=10.242.2.3  sport=23628  dport=8000
```

Source IP is **`192.168.65.1`** — Docker Desktop's VM gateway, NOT the enclave bridge gateway, NOT inside `10.242.2.0/24`. Bottle's listener received the SYN, sent a SYN-ACK, and never got the ACK. Connection stuck at SYN-RECV.

But sentry's FORWARD chain shows **0 packets** — sentry never saw the SYN-ACK. So the bottle's SYN-ACK either didn't leave the bottle, or got lost before reaching sentry's interface. Let me check the bottle's neighbor/ARP state.

input.command:
echo "=== pentacle neighbor table ===" && docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle ip neigh
echo ""
echo "=== pentacle route get 192.168.65.1 (where would SYN-ACK go?) ==="
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle ip route get 192.168.65.1
echo ""
echo "=== bridge from host: enclave network gateway info ==="
docker network inspect canrbhm-srjcl_enclave --format '{{json .IPAM.Config}}'
echo ""
echo "=== Test: is sentry reachable from pentacle via ping (uses ICMP allow)? ==="
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle ping -c 2 -W 1 10.242.2.2 2>&1 | tail -5
echo ""
echo "=== After ping — pentacle neighbor ==="
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle ip neigh
echo ""
echo "=== Test: from bottle, can we curl pentacle->bottle's own listener? ==="
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-bottle curl --max-time 3 -s -o /dev/null -w 'HTTP %{http_code}\n' http://localhost:8000/ 2>&1 | head -5

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Debian bookworm-slim for minimal trust boundary sentry
ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Avoid prompts from apt
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install sentry operational packages + pentacle diagnostic packages
# (pentacle container shares this image for trusted network observation)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    bash            `# Shell for sentry setup script`       \
    iptables        `# Firewall rule configuration`         \
    dnsmasq         `# DNS forwarding for enclave`          \
    iproute2        `# Network configuration (ip command)`  \
    iputils-ping    `# ICMP tests (pentacle role)`            \
    dnsutils        `# dig/nslookup (pentacle role)`          \
    procps          `# pidof/ps for process inspection`       \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Pin iptables-legacy backend (nftables not available in slim)
RUN update-alternatives --set iptables /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy

# Bake entrypoint scripts (sentry setup + pentacle routing)
# Source of truth: Tools/rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh, Tools/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh
# Changes require deliberate build→vouch cycle
COPY rbjs_sentry.sh  /opt/rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh
COPY rbjp_pentacle.sh /opt/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh
RUN chmod +x /opt/rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh /opt/rbk/rbjp_pentacle.sh

# Default: sentry entrypoint (pentacle overrides via compose command)
CMD ["/opt/rbk/rbjs_sentry.sh"]

lines: # Pin iptables-legacy backend (nftables not available in slim)

input.params: {"identity": "A_AAb", "files": ["rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/Dockerfile"], "intent": "Add tcpdump to the sentry/pentacle image so scry (Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh, tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh) actually works. Discovered during the pentacle-publisher diagnostic: scry's tcpdump exec failed with 'No such file or directory' because tcpdump was never in the image apt-install list. The scry tooling appears to have been written against an assumption that tcpdump would be present, but the Dockerfile installs only bash/iptables/dnsmasq/iproute2/iputils-ping/dnsutils/procps. Adding tcpdump completes the existing diagnostic infrastructure. Without it the only probes available are iptables counter deltas, ss state inspection, and /proc/net/nf_conntrack — which got us to 'connection in SYN_RECV state with src=192.168.65.1' but cannot show packet-level flow at sentry's interfaces. Next: kludge sentry vessel and refresh srjcl hallmark to test the new image."}

input.command:
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-pentacle timeout 10 tcpdump -U -l -nn -i any -c 60 'tcp port 8000 or tcp port 7999 or tcp port 23628 or icmp' > /tmp/scry-pentacle.txt 2>&1 &
PEN=$!
docker exec canrbhm-srjcl-sentry   timeout 10 tcpdump -U -l -nn -i any -c 60 'tcp or icmp' > /tmp/scry-sentry.txt 2>&1 &
SEN=$!
sleep 2
echo "[curl probe]"
curl --connect-timeout 3 --max-time 5 -v http://localhost:7999/lab 2>&1 | grep -E '(Trying|Connected|HTTP|timed out|Operation)' | head -10
sleep 4
wait $PEN 2>/dev/null
wait $SEN 2>/dev/null
echo ""
echo "=== PENTACLE TCPDUMP (any iface, filtered) ==="
cat /tmp/scry-pentacle.txt
echo ""
echo "=== SENTRY TCPDUMP (any iface, all TCP+ICMP) ==="
cat /tmp/scry-sentry.txt

content.subject: Scry diagnostic on srjcl failing path
content.description: Charge srjcl, capture pre-state, run parallel tcpdumps inside pentacle+sentry during a curl probe, capture post-state, quench. Report observed source IP, packet path, listener state, and routing.

content.subject: Add tcpdump to sentry image
content.description: Edit common-sentry-context/Dockerfile to install tcpdump alongside the other tools. Notch, kludge vessel, KludgeSentry srjcl, notch, quench, re-charge.

content.text:
**Definitive empirical confirmation of the failure mechanism — and an architectural realization.**

The tcpdump captures show:

**Pentacle eth0 (bottle's namespace):**
```
eth0  In   192.168.65.1:54174  >  10.242.2.3:8000   [S]   ← SYN from Docker Desktop host gateway
eth0  Out  10.242.2.3:8000     >  192.168.65.1:54174 [S.] ← bottle's SYN-ACK
                                                            (repeats on curl retries — never gets ACK)
```

**Sentry eth1 (transit interface):**
```
eth1  In   192.168.65.3:7999   >  192.168.65.1:54174 [S.] ← SYN-ACK after Docker Desktop SNAT
                                                            (source rewritten from 10.242.2.3:8000 → 192.168.65.3:7999)
```

**Sentry FORWARD chain counters: ALL zero.** Sentry's iptables never processed the SYN-ACK as a forward operation. The packet appears on sentry's transit interface but doesn't traverse FORWARD — likely because Docker Desktop's networking layer is reflecting/handling it outside sentry's iptables hook order.

**What this tells us — and where I was wrong:**

The source IP is `192.168.65.1` (Docker Desktop's host-to-VM gateway), NOT inside the enclave CIDR. So the source-IP-exclusion rule from attempt 2 would have ALLOWED the SYN — my earlier hypothesis ("source IP is the enclave bridge gateway") was incorrect.

The actual failure mechanism is what the architect's hypothesis 5 described, but cleaner than the routing-table-damage variant: **the bottle's default-route-via-sentry forces return packets toward sentry; Docker Desktop's NAT then bounces the SNAT'd return through sentry's transit network; sentry has no FORWARD rule that matches `dst=192.168.65.0/24`, so the packet is dropped (or otherwise lost).**

**The architectural conclusion:**

The pentacle-publisher topology cannot work *with* the bottle's default-route-via-sentry, because the bottle cannot route reply traffic to the host without sentry, and sentry isn't a router for the host's Docker Desktop range.

**More importantly — this RE-VALIDATES the ORIGINAL DESIGN.** The pre-regression design had sentry doing DNAT + MASQUERADE on entry-port traffic precisely because the MASQUERADE made the bottle's reply go *back to sentry* (consistent with default-route), where sentry's conntrack unwound the NAT and the reply went out the way the SYN came in. That's a *symmetric* path that doesn't require the bottle to know how to reach the original client.

The bug was never the architecture. The bug was the iptables CLASSIFIER (`-i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}`) — a brittle proxy for "outside the enclave" that broke when Docker started delivering via a different interface. The memo's Refined Fix Shape (source-CIDR-exclusion on PREROUTING + conntrack-DNAT-state on FORWARD + keep MASQUERADE) is the right fix.

**Why attempt 2 stage 2 (source-IP-exclusion, no rename) also failed** is still uncertain — given that source IP is `192.168.65.1` (outside enclave CIDR), the source-CIDR exclusion *should* have allowed it. Possibilities:
- The return-path was broken for a different reason in attempt 2 (need re-diagnosis if we go back).
- The FORWARD chain's source-CIDR exclusion was also blocking *post-DNAT* traffic somehow.
- The conntrack-DNAT-state on FORWARD (which we never tested) is required and source-CIDR on FORWARD isn't enough.

Diagnostic crucible quenched. The findings are summarized above; the experiment commits are still on disk waiting for your decision on the path forward (most likely: revert the topology reframe, return to sentry-as-publisher, but replace the broken `-i UPLINK_IF` classifier with the conntrack-state form rather than the source-CIDR form that we now know was the right shape but with the wrong implementation).

Holding for your direction.

content: Diagnosing the srjcl Jupyter port-publish regression in heat ₣A_; pentacle-publisher reframe just failed empirically because the bottle's default-route-via-sentry breaks the return path. Next: revert the topology reframe and try the memo's conntrack-DNAT-state form with sentry as publisher.

message.content: All right, the architect is processing your findings.  We will want to 'revert' some/ all of the changes we have made in this debug session.  Would you assess these changes and prepare a prompt asking our architect which aspects to retain and which ones to revert?

00595a26 jjb:1011-9eb7588d:₣A_:S: sentry-entry-port-dnat-forward-rescope

65359863 jjb:1011-c42009d5:₢A_AAb:n: EXPERIMENT (revert after observation): rename compose network 'transit' → 'auplink' so it sorts alphabetically before 'enclave'. Memo memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md asserts Docker's port-publish target is the alphabetically-first attached network — extrapolated from BBABE's empirically-established alphabetical rule for default-route assignment, but not independently verified for port-publish. This experiment falsifies-or-confirms that extrapolation as a binary outcome: if alphabetical hypothesis holds, srjcl jupyter-connectivity goes green (HTTP 200) with the renamed network because the shipped DNAT (-i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}) and RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT (-i uplink -o enclave) rules now match the path Docker actually delivers packets on; if false, curl stays at HTTP 000 and we need a different theory of the regression. Two files touched: (1) .rbk/rbob_compose.yml renames the network at top-level declaration (line 99) and at sentry's services.networks ref (line 33), plus header-comment update; (2) Tools/rbk/rbob_bottle.sh:345 collision-guard allowlist swaps 'transit' → 'auplink' so the guard recognises the renamed network as ours. No image rebuild required — sentry's rbjs_sentry.sh does interface discovery by IP (against RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP), never reads docker network names, so the baked entrypoint is unaffected. Test: tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.srjcl.sh; expected to flip from HTTP 000 (broken in baseline) to HTTP 200 if alphabetical-ordering hypothesis is causal for port-publish target selection. Edits are surgical and explicitly tagged with ₢A_AAb experiment marker in both files for trivial revertability.
3a089290 jjb:1011-65359863:₢A_AAb:n: EXPERIMENT continuation (revert after observation): restore srjcl's RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC from 120 back to 30 (the original value introduced in ₢BBAAt commit 0a850d57). The 120 bump in ₢A_AAP commit 28d1411f was a 'Jupyter takes longer to bind' hypothesis from before the port-publish-routing root cause was understood. Now that the compose-rename experiment confirms the regression was a routing problem and not a readiness problem, we predict 30s is still sufficient because Jupyter has always bound its listener well within 30s — the curl was failing because DNAT didn't match the delivered packet, not because Jupyter wasn't listening. This re-run at delay=30 with the rename in place either: (a) passes — confirming the routing fix is robust at the original timing budget and the 120 bump was masking; or (b) fails on the connectivity case — meaning there's a genuine cold-conjure readiness issue independent of routing, in which case the 120 bump would need to land permanently regardless of routing fix choice. Required nameplate-side commit per the charge tabtarget's uncommitted-changes guard.
09fa78da jjb:1011-3a089290:₢A_AAb:n: Apply source-IP-exclusion repair to sentry's entry-port DNAT and RBM-FORWARD rules. Replaces the broken -i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF} interface proxy with the actual semantic invariant: ! -s ${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}. Three reasons this form is correct rather than the memo's bare -i drop: (1) the active ifrit sortie net-dnat-entry-reflection (registered at rbida_attacks.rs:344, dispatched in tadmor at rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2252) asserts that enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach the entry port through sentry's DNAT — the shipped -i UPLINK_IF rule passed this only by interface accident, and a bare -i drop would silently regress the case; source-IP exclusion preserves the invariant explicitly in nameplate vocabulary. (2) Syntax precedent exists in the same script at line 154-156 where MASQUERADE uses ! -d on the same CIDR variables, so the new rules introduce no new patterns. (3) Extends BBABE's 'sentry takes ownership of its networking' precedent to the entry-port half: instead of trusting Docker's choice of port-publish delivery interface (alphabetical fallback per the compose header comment), the rule expresses what it actually means — entry-port traffic originating outside the enclave subnet — using only project-controlled constants. srjcl and pluml share the same sentry vessel + hallmark (c260511164345-r260511234349), so one kludge cycle covers both. Comment added to the rule block points at rbob_compose.yml's header for context on why the proxy was wrong. Next steps: kludge sentry vessel, refresh hallmarks on both nameplates, run srjcl fixture (expect green: rename + fix), run tadmor suite (regression gate for net-dnat-entry-reflection), then revert the experimental rename and re-run srjcl (proves fix is rename-independent — the renaming-no-impact proof of correctness).
257a027f jjb:1011-09fa78da:₢A_AAb:n: User-authored research import: Post-Diagnosis Research section added to the regression memo. Committing as-is at user direction to clear the kludge guard's clean-tree precondition. Content is the user's; not reviewed line-by-line in this commit.
1522dab7 jjb:1011-257a027f:₢A_AAb:n: Refresh srjcl sentry hallmark to k260512080911-257a027f after kludging rbev-sentry-deb-tether with the source-IP-exclusion DNAT/FORWARD rules. Required to commit before continuing to pluml's hallmark refresh (kludge guard refuses dirty tree).
9252ba2d jjb:1011-1522dab7:₢A_AAb:n: Refresh pluml sentry hallmark to k260512080941-1522dab7. Same underlying source-IP-exclusion rbjs_sentry.sh; pluml's hallmark identifier differs from srjcl's only because each kludge cycle produces a fresh timestamped identifier. Pluml and srjcl now both carry the corrected entry-port DNAT/FORWARD rules.

77615971 jjb:1011-a542ca7c:₢A_AAb:n: Revert the experimental transit→auplink rename. Stage 1 verification (rename + source-IP-exclusion fix together) showed all three fixtures green: srjcl 3/3, pluml 5/5, tadmor 54/54 including the rbtdrc_sortie_net_dnat_entry_reflection regression gate. Now we revert the rename to verify the fix is rename-independent — the 'renaming has no impact' proof. With this revert, Docker will go back to delivering port-publish via the enclave interface (alphabetically-first), and the source-IP-exclusion DNAT must allow it through purely on source-IP-not-in-enclave-CIDR grounds. If stage 2 stays green, the fix has proven its independence from Docker's interface-selection heuristic — which was the entire point of choosing source-IP-exclusion over the memo's bare -i drop or compose-rename alternatives. The .rbk/rbob_compose.yml and Tools/rbk/rbob_bottle.sh contents are now byte-identical to their pre-experiment state (just before commit 65359863); only the experiment markers in commit history remain.
40c8d78e jjb:1011-77615971:₢A_AAb:n: Capture two rounds of web research into the memo's Post-Diagnosis Research section. First round established documented-vs-observed status for the original presumptions (Docker's alphabetical port-publish target selection is empirical-only, `default`-name magic is not documented as primacy, Compose `priority:` does not select port-publish target, long-form `ports:` exposes no `container_ip`, macOS Desktop hairpin DROP is observed-only); identified Podman rootless source-IP rewriting through rootlesskit (`10.0.2.100`) as load-bearing for any source-CIDR-based fix; surfaced `gw_priority` (Compose 2.33.1+) as a Compose-level alternative to BBABE's sentry-side default-route override. Second round source-traced Docker's alphabetical behavior through compose-go `NetworksByPriority()` (priority + lexicographic tie-break) and Docker Compose's `primaryNetworkKey` derivation in `pkg/compose/create.go` — chain traced up to the final 'Compose primary network → port-publish DNAT target' hop, which remains undocumented; source-traced Netavark's DNAT chain emission via `src/firewall/nft.rs`; established `iptables-extensions(8)` `-m conntrack --ctstate DNAT` virtual state as a documented mainline netfilter primitive providing a strictly stronger FORWARD invariant than source-CIDR exclusion. Refined Fix Shape upgrades RBM-FORWARD from source-CIDR exclusion to conntrack DNAT-state matching (PREROUTING retains source-CIDR as classification predicate since no conntrack state exists yet at PREROUTING time); expands rootless overlap guardrail from one CIDR to three (`10.0.2.100/32`, `10.0.2.0/24`, `127.0.0.0/8`) with rationale; adds runtime source-IP probe because Docker Engine and Desktop do not document target-container source-IP visibility; flags IPv6 published-port ingress as out-of-scope but tracked. Adds Source-Rewrite Address Visibility cross-runtime table and Refined Fix Per-Runtime Coverage table covering nine runtime contexts. Updates Open Empirical Questions (items 1/3/4 partially-resolved or documented; adds new items for Docker Engine/Desktop source-IP visibility, IPv6 behavior, post-restart reachability). Extends References with 14 primary-source URLs from the two research rounds. Memo principle ('sentry takes ownership; don't trust the runtime's selection') and conclusion path (cross-platform verification gate non-negotiable) unchanged; the research refines the concrete iptables shape and adds operational guardrails.
05a9787b jjb:1011-40c8d78e:₢A_AAb:n: Incorporate Round 4 research into the memo's pentacle-publisher topology section. Round 4 (cross-runtime robustness investigation) validates the topology with two operationalizable caveats: (1) lifecycle coupling — pentacle-recreated-alone can break bottle's joined network namespace per docker/compose#10263; mitigation is treat pentacle+bottle as one lifecycle unit (`docker compose restart pentacle bottle`, possibly enforced via rbob_charge/rbob_quench guards); (2) listener source-IP non-portability — the topology fixes publish-target determinism, NOT source-IP fidelity, so bottle's listener sees runtime-dependent peer addresses (bridge gateway / proxy / rootlesskit `10.0.2.100` / pasta-preserved / etc.); if original-client IP ever matters for future entry-mode use cases, add an application-layer proxy with PROXY protocol or `X-Forwarded-For`. Replaces the placeholder 'Cross-runtime portability' subsection with a full validity matrix (Docker Compose v2/Engine supported; `ports:` on namespace guest rejected at engine level as 'conflicting options: port publishing and the container type network mode' — defensive property; Docker Desktop supported; `podman compose` provider-dependent; `podman-compose` Python supported-with-caveat; Podman native pod strongly supported with explicit 'You must not publish ports of containers in the pod individually, but only by the pod itself'). Adds listener source-IP table covering all nine runtime/proxy contexts. Adds Round 4-recommended security envelope refinement: drop `CAP_NET_ADMIN` from bottle (current `cap_drop: [NET_RAW]` is insufficient for the namespace-sharing topology — without NET_ADMIN drop, bottle could alter pentacle's port-publish rules in the shared netns). Adds pod-equivalent posture comparison (Kubernetes pods use same model intentionally; Compose `network_mode: service:X` is single-host approximation; for Docker-first deployment Compose form is more portable; for Podman-native deployment a Podman pod is semantically cleaner; project keeps Compose as primary, evaluates Podman-pod variant later as future-work). Adds four empirical observations to the experiment protocol (Round 4 specified): (A) confirm guest-publishing rejection via `docker inspect ... PortBindings` showing bottle null/empty and pentacle expected; (B) record source-IP visibility at bottle's listener via tcpdump for cross-runtime data; (C) probe pentacle-restart-alone behavior to confirm/refute docker/compose#10263 hazard in our deployment context; (D) observe startup window (port advertised before listener bound) to validate whether the existing `RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC=120` on srjcl is still needed post-reframe or can be reduced. Adds Step 3 to experiment protocol: optional same-change-set addition of NET_ADMIN to bottle's `cap_drop`. Updates Hypothesis posture from 'architecturally strong but empirically pending' to 'research-validated with two operationalizable caveats, empirically pending'. Extends References with 7 new Round 4 URLs (Compose Spec, docker/compose#10263, Compose create.go raw, podman-compose(1), containers/podman-compose, podman-run(1), Kubernetes networking concepts).
977456ae jjb:1011-05a9787b:₢A_AAb:n: Correct the memo's experiment protocol in response to coding-agent clarifying questions. (1) Port mapping was wrong: said the directive value was 'unchanged' (7999:7999), but without sentry's PREROUTING DNAT translating 7999→8000(/8080), the publish itself must translate. Corrected to asymmetric form `"${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}"` — for srjcl `7999:8000`, for pluml `7999:8080`. (2) Added new Step 3: lift `ip_forward=1` enablement out of the entry-port block in rbjs_sentry.sh to an unconditional earlier line, so kernel-forward state is independent of which access-mode iptables block runs. The current entry-port block at line 121-122 enables ip_forward as a side-effect; deletion leaves pluml (access_mode=disabled) with ip_forward=0 because pluml's access-mode block doesn't touch ip_forward. Lifting normalizes kernel state across access modes; functional behavior unchanged (pluml outbound remains iptables-blocked). (3) Expanded hallmark refresh from 'srjcl and pluml' to 'every nameplate exercised in the test iteration' — sentry vessel is shared across srjcl/pluml/tadmor/moriah/ccyolo, so any unrefreshed nameplate in the iteration runs against the OLD sentry image and its pass/fail validates the old image rather than the new one. For the three-fixture iteration (srjcl + pluml + tadmor), refresh all three. (4) Split verification into iteration-cycle (three-fixture, early signal) and canonical-cycle (full pristine gauntlet on both platforms, declaration gate). Memo principle and topology reframe conclusion unchanged; this is housekeeping on the experiment protocol following coding-agent triage.
c8b218ef jjb:1011-977456ae:₢A_AAb:n: Topology-level reframe of the entry port: move port publish from sentry (dual-network) to pentacle (single-network), and delete the entry-port iptables block from sentry. The architectural flaw being repaired: making sentry the publisher coupled the gatekeeper role with a multi-network disambiguation problem that has no portable Compose/runtime API. Pentacle is single-attached (enclave only); Docker has one network to target, no disambiguation. Pentacle and bottle share a network namespace, so the publish lands on the bottle's listener directly. Compose change: ports directive moves from sentry to pentacle with asymmetric form ${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE} (host 7999 → in-namespace 8000 for srjcl, 7999 → 8080 for pluml). The asymmetric form is necessary because without sentry's PREROUTING DNAT the publish itself must carry the port translation. Sentry script change: the entire entry-mode if-block at lines 117-142 deleted (PREROUTING DNAT, RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT, POSTROUTING MASQUERADE — all three become moot when sentry is not in the inbound path). ip_forward enablement was previously latent in the entry-mode block; lifted to an unconditional position at RBJp2 since sentry forwards on behalf of the enclave whenever access mode permits, independent of entry mode. Removed the redundant ip_forward set in the access-mode-enabled branch (was duplicated). Security posture: ifrit's net-dnat-entry-reflection invariant ('enclave-internal sources MUST NOT reach sentry entry port') is preserved trivially — sentry no longer has an entry port. Sentry's egress-gatekeeper role unchanged (default route, DNS, FORWARD/EGRESS rules for outbound traffic all untouched). Next: kludge sentry vessel, refresh hallmarks on srjcl + pluml + tadmor (all three exercised in parallel verification), run the three fixtures in parallel.
8798c6a4 jjb:1011-c8b218ef:₢A_AAb:n: Refresh srjcl sentry hallmark to k260512090035-c8b218ef carrying the pentacle-publisher topology reframe (entry-port iptables block deleted, ip_forward lifted).
3c5bac2d jjb:1011-8798c6a4:₢A_AAb:n: Refresh pluml sentry hallmark to k260512090045-8798c6a4 (pentacle-publisher topology image).
ba8518fc jjb:1011-3c5bac2d:₢A_AAb:n: Refresh tadmor sentry hallmark to k260512090057-3c5bac2d (pentacle-publisher topology image) — so tadmor's adversarial security suite validates egress posture under the NEW sentry script, not the previous one.
bd33909c jjb:1011-ba8518fc:₢A_AAb:n: Add tcpdump to the sentry/pentacle image so scry (Tools/rbk/rboo_observe.sh, tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh) actually works. Discovered during the pentacle-publisher diagnostic: scry's tcpdump exec failed with 'No such file or directory' because tcpdump was never in the image apt-install list. The scry tooling appears to have been written against an assumption that tcpdump would be present, but the Dockerfile installs only bash/iptables/dnsmasq/iproute2/iputils-ping/dnsutils/procps. Adding tcpdump completes the existing diagnostic infrastructure. Without it the only probes available are iptables counter deltas, ss state inspection, and /proc/net/nf_conntrack — which got us to 'connection in SYN_RECV state with src=192.168.65.1' but cannot show packet-level flow at sentry's interfaces. content.text:
# Debug Session Change Inventory — for Architect's Retain/Revert Decision

## Commits in this session (₢A_AAb)

16 commits between `65359863` and `e73fb2ce`. By file category:

---

## Category 1 — Source-IP-Exclusion attempt (attempt 2)

**Status: superseded by attempt 3 (topology reframe) in the source tree, but the iptables shape is the memo's Refined Fix Shape building block.**

- `09fa78da` — `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`: replaced `-i ${RBJ_UPLINK_IF}` with `! -s "${RBRN_ENCLAVE_BASE_IP}/${RBRN_ENCLAVE_NETMASK}"` on PREROUTING DNAT and RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT. Memo's Refined Fix Shape later upgraded RBM-FORWARD to `-m conntrack --ctstate DNAT`; we never implemented that variant.

**Architect decision:** which iptables shape should land canonically?
- A. Full revert to original `-i UPLINK_IF` (pre-session baseline)
- B. Source-CIDR-exclusion as committed in `09fa78da` (attempt 2)
- C. Memo's Refined Fix Shape: source-CIDR on PREROUTING + conntrack-DNAT-state on FORWARD (never tested by us)
- D. Original architecture, but with rename + alphabetical-ordering as documented load-bearing fact

The empirical failure mode of attempt 3 reaffirmed sentry-as-publisher is the correct architecture. The remaining question is the classifier shape on the sentry-side rules.

---

## Category 2 — Topology Reframe attempt (attempt 3)

**Status: empirically broken on macOS Docker Desktop. Recommend revert.**

- `c8b218ef` — two files:
  - `.rbk/rbob_compose.yml`: moved `ports:` directive from sentry to pentacle, asymmetric form `${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_WORKSTATION}:${RBRN_ENTRY_PORT_ENCLAVE}`
  - `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/rbjs_sentry.sh`: deleted the entire entry-port iptables block (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT + MASQUERADE); lifted `ip_forward=1` to unconditional position at RBJp2; removed redundant `ip_forward` set in access-mode-enabled branch

**The compose change and the entry-port-block deletion must revert** — they're load-bearing for the return-path symmetry that the original design provided via MASQUERADE.

**The `ip_forward` lift is a hygiene improvement** that's orthogonal to the architecture choice. Architect: retain the lift as a follow-up cleanup, or revert it as part of returning to baseline?

---

## Category 3 — tcpdump in sentry image

**Status: independently useful. Recommend retain.**

- `bd33909c` — `rbev-vessels/common-sentry-context/Dockerfile`: added `tcpdump` to the apt-install list.

This made `tt/rbw-cs.Scry.sh` actually functional (it was broken — tcpdump was never installed despite being referenced by scry). Independent of any iptables/topology decision.

**Architect decision:** retain (recommended), or strip if image-size discipline demands?

---

## Category 4 — Readiness delay revert

**Status: undid a misdiagnosis from ₢A_AAP. Recommend retain (i.e., leave at 30).**

- `3a089290` — `.rbk/srjcl/rbrn.env`: `RBRN_BOTTLE_READINESS_DELAY_SEC=120 → 30` (back to the original from ₢BBAAt). The 120-bump in ₢A_AAP (commit `28d1411f`) was a "Jupyter takes longer to bind" hypothesis that turned out wrong — connectivity failure was routing, not readiness. Diagnostic confirmed Jupyter binds 0.0.0.0:8000 well within 30s.

**Architect decision:** is there independent reason to bump back to 120 (cold-conjure timing budget for pristine gauntlet)? Or keep at 30?

---

## Category 5 — Compose network rename experiment

**Status: already reverted in commit `77615971`. No action needed.**

- `65359863` — renamed `transit → auplink`; `77615971` — reverted. The empirical experiment confirmed the alphabetical-ordering mechanism; net change to source tree is zero.

**Architect decision:** none. (Unless we adopt option D in Category 1, in which case we'd re-apply the rename as a *documented* load-bearing convention rather than experimental.)

---

## Category 6 — Nameplate hallmark refreshes

**Status: ALL currently point at broken/diagnostic images. Will need re-refresh once Category 1 lands.**

- `.rbk/srjcl/rbrn.env` `RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK`: currently `k260512092705-bd33909c` (the tcpdump-equipped reframed image — broken topology).
- `.rbk/pluml/rbrn.env` `RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK`: currently `k260512090045-8798c6a4` (reframed image — broken topology, no tcpdump).
- `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env` `RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK`: currently `k260512090057-3c5bac2d` (reframed image — broken topology, no tcpdump).

**Mechanical follow-up:** after Categories 1+2+3 land in their final form, re-kludge sentry once and refresh all three nameplate hallmarks. No standalone decision needed.

---

## Category 7 — Memo (user-authored research)

**Status: user-authored. Retain as-is.**

- `257a027f`, `40c8d78e`, `05a9787b`, `977456ae` — incremental updates to `Memos/memo-20260512-srjcl-port-publish-regression.md`. Cumulative content includes: Post-Diagnosis Research section, cross-runtime portability matrix, Source-Rewrite Address Visibility table, Refined Fix Shape, runtime probe, multi-CIDR guardrail, pentacle-publisher topology section, Round-4 research validation.

The empirical findings from THIS session (`192.168.65.1` source IP, sentry FORWARD chain bypass, etc.) are not yet in the memo. Architect may want a memo-update commit that captures the empirical falsification of the topology reframe before committing the actual repair.

---

## Decisions needed (concise)

1. **Iptables fix shape** (Category 1): A / B / C / D? This drives the sentry-script content + hallmark refreshes.
2. **`ip_forward` lift hygiene** (Category 2, partial): retain the unconditional-lift refactor, or revert entirely?
3. **tcpdump in image** (Category 3): retain (default) or strip?
4. **Readiness delay** (Category 4): keep 30, or restore 120 for pristine-gauntlet cold-conjure margin?
5. **Memo update with this session's empirical findings**: should I draft an addendum capturing the pentacle-publisher falsification, or leave that for the architect to write?

Once you give me 1-5, the revert/re-implement sequence is mechanical: edit → notch → kludge → refresh hallmarks → run three-fixture parallel verification.

message.content:
Answers below: proceed.



  1. Iptables fix shape — none of A/B/C/D. The answer is the anchor memo's recipe.

  Read Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md — specifically the "Fix recipe" section. The shape is:

  - PREROUTING DNAT classified by destination port only. No interface filter, no source-IP filter. Differs from B and C which
  include source-CIDR; the anchor memo's analysis (and the scry evidence) shows source-CIDR is unnecessary overconstraint on Docker
   Desktop (source 192.168.65.1 is outside enclave CIDR anyway) AND structurally broken on rootless Podman (rootlesskit's
  10.0.2.100 rewrite). Drop it entirely.
  - POSTROUTING MASQUERADE retained on the entry-port path. This is what B and C both missed. The scry-empirical falsification of
  attempt 3 confirmed MASQUERADE is structurally load-bearing (keeps bottle's reply destination in-enclave for clean
  conntrack-mediated return).
  - RBM-FORWARD ACCEPT matched on destination + -m conntrack --ctstate DNAT. The Round 3 form; correct on its own terms.
  - RBM-FORWARD baseline ESTABLISHED,RELATED rule — verify presence in rbjs_sentry.sh; add if absent. This is likely the missing
  piece in attempt 2 stage 2's failure (the return path needs explicit FORWARD authorization separate from the inbound
  conntrack-DNAT rule).

  Call this option E if you need a label. It's interface-agnostic AND source-IP-agnostic (good for Docker Engine, Docker Desktop,
  Podman rootful, Podman rootless all uniformly), with MASQUERADE preserving the return-path symmetry that all of A/B/C variants
  depended on but only A explicitly retained. It's the principled outcome of three attempts: BBABE's "sentry takes ownership"
  applied consistently to both classification (destination port, the stable trust label) and authorization (conntrack state, the
  unforgeable invariant).

  Option D rejected definitively. Cross-runtime mandate makes "documented alphabetical convention" a non-starter —
  alphabetical-first is empirical-only on Docker, no guarantee on Podman netavark, breaks the project's anti-ordering discipline.

  2. ip_forward lift — retain.

  It's independent hygiene that fixed a latent coupling between iptables installation and kernel state. Listed explicitly in the
  anchor memo's "What to RETAIN from the failed attempt." The fact that it surfaced as a side-effect of the failed reframe doesn't
  make it less correct.

  3. tcpdump in sentry image — retain.

  Makes the rbw-cs.Scry.sh tabtarget actually functional, which is what produced the decisive diagnostic evidence. Image-size
  discipline isn't load-bearing here — tcpdump is a few MB; the diagnostic capability is permanent institutional value. Listed in
  the anchor memo's retain list.

  4. Readiness delay — keep at 30.

  The 120 bump in ₢A_AAP was a misdiagnosis: the AAP gauntlet's HTTP 000 failure looked like a readiness window from the outside,
  but the bottle was actually listening within 30s. The connectivity failure was routing (interface-name DNAT match failing
  post-BBABC), not readiness. Reverting to 30 is correct.

  There's one second-order consideration worth flagging but not blocking on: if the canonical fix lands and cold-conjure
  pristine-gauntlet runs reveal 30 is occasionally tight on slower hardware, bump locally. But there's no reason to preemptively
  bump back to 120 — the actual readiness data point came from empirical observation, not theoretical worst-case.

  Worth a one-line companion-memo note that the 120 was retrospectively a misdiagnosis (the diagnosis lineage is part of the
  lessons), but that's optional cleanup, not blocking.

  5. Memo update — not needed from the coder.

  The anchor memo (Memos/memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md) already captures this session's empirical findings — the
   scry data (192.168.65.1, RBM-FORWARD counters zero, SYN-ACK reflection through transit), the architectural conclusion
  (MASQUERADE load-bearing), the falsification of the topology reframe. That's why I wrote it.

  The companion memo doesn't need an update for these findings — the anchor memo IS the canonical record of post-reframe-failure
  state. The companion stays as historical lineage. The two memos compose: anchor for action, companion for lineage.

  If you want one optional cleanup: a single-line note in the companion memo's "Topology-Level Reframing" section saying
  "empirically refuted; see memo-20260512-sentry-entry-port-repair-anchor.md for action plan." But strictly optional — the
  cross-reference in the anchor memo's "What is now superseded" section handles the same job from the other direction. I'd skip it
  for tightness.

  Execution sequence after these decisions

  Mechanical, as you described:

  1. Edit rbob_compose.yml — ports: back to sentry, symmetric 7999:7999, retain NET_ADMIN in bottle's cap_drop: (the anchor memo's
  retain).
  2. Edit rbjs_sentry.sh — re-introduce the entry-port block per the anchor memo's recipe. Read the file's current state for
  variable names and surrounding rule order. Verify ESTABLISHED,RELATED in RBM-FORWARD; add if absent.
  3. Notch with intent describing the revert-to-anchor-fix sequence.
  4. Kludge sentry once.
  5. Refresh hallmarks on srjcl + pluml + tadmor.
  6. Run scry while issuing a single curl probe — verify the symmetric return path per the anchor memo's Step 3 (bottle sees
  sentry's enclave IP as source via MASQUERADE; bottle's SYN-ACK stays in-enclave; sentry's RBM-FORWARD counters increment;
  conntrack reverses NAT; curl gets HTTP 200).
  7. Three-fixture iteration (srjcl + pluml + tadmor) on this platform.
  8. Coordinate cross-platform verification with linux operator.
  9. Once cross-platform green, full pristine gauntlet for canonical pass.

  You're set. The anchor memo has everything you need; this answer is just the green-light pass on what to revert vs retain. After
  this iteration runs (green or red), the next mount picks up the result and decides next steps from there.

  Good work on the inventory. Decisive answers above; proceed.
